Contents

  • 1 Introduction
  • 2 Data Loading & Preparation
  • 3 Where Are Newborns Dying? — World Map
  • 4 The Top 10 Burden Countries — Bar Chart
  • 5 Adolescent Population vs. Neonatal Deaths — Scatterplot
  • 6 Time-Series: Progress Over Decades
  • 7 Conclusion

The Silent Crisis: Neonatal Deaths & the World’s Adolescent Population

Author

Julie Bazerolle

Published

April 13, 2026

1 Introduction

Every year, millions of newborns take their first — and last — breath within just 28 days of life. Neonatal deaths remain one of the most preventable tragedies in global public health, yet they disproportionately burden the world’s poorest nations. At the same time, the global adolescent population (ages 10–19) is expanding rapidly, concentrated in the very same regions where newborn survival rates are lowest.

This report weaves together two UNICEF datasets to tell a single, urgent story:

  • Where are neonatal deaths most concentrated?
  • Which countries carry the heaviest burden in absolute terms?
  • How has progress (or regression) unfolded over time?
  • Is there a relationship between a country’s adolescent population size and its neonatal death toll?

Data sources: UNICEF global indicators — Neonatal Deaths (indicator 2) and Adolescent Population 10–19 (indicator 1), enriched with country-level metadata (population, GDP per capita, life expectancy).

2 Data Loading & Preparation

Code 
import subprocess
subprocess.run(["pip", "install", "-q", "plotnine", "polars", "geopandas"], check=False)

import polars as pl
import numpy as np
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes, geom_polygon, geom_col, geom_point, geom_line,
    geom_smooth, geom_text, coord_flip,
    scale_fill_manual, scale_color_manual, scale_size_continuous,
    labs, theme, theme_minimal, theme_void, element_text,
    element_blank, element_rect, element_line,
    scale_x_continuous, scale_y_continuous,
    scale_fill_distiller, annotate
)
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# ── Load raw files ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ind1 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")   # Adolescent population
ind2 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")   # Neonatal deaths
meta = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")

# ── Quick EDA ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("Indicator 1 shape:", ind1.shape)
print("Indicator 2 shape:", ind2.shape)
print("Metadata shape   :", meta.shape)
print("\nNeonatal deaths — obs_value summary:")
print(ind2.select("obs_value").describe())
Indicator 1 shape: (50616, 14)
Indicator 2 shape: (9962, 14)
Metadata shape   : (13715, 15)

Neonatal deaths — obs_value summary:
shape: (9, 2)
┌────────────┬───────────────┐
│ statistic  ┆ obs_value     │
│ ---        ┆ ---           │
│ str        ┆ f64           │
╞════════════╪═══════════════╡
│ count      ┆ 9962.0        │
│ null_count ┆ 0.0           │
│ mean       ┆ 23261.529512  │
│ std        ┆ 110749.805497 │
│ min        ┆ 0.0           │
│ 25%        ┆ 286.0         │
│ 50%        ┆ 2295.0        │
│ 75%        ┆ 13889.0       │
│ max        ┆ 1.896003e6    │
└────────────┴───────────────┘
Code 
# ── 1. Neonatal deaths: keep Total sex, rename for clarity ───────────────────
neo = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "time_period", "obs_value"])
    .rename({"obs_value": "neonatal_deaths", "time_period": "year"})
)

# ── 2. Adolescent population: keep Total sex + Total age ─────────────────────
adol = (
    ind1
    .filter((pl.col("sex") == "Total") & (pl.col("current_age") == "Total"))
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "time_period", "obs_value"])
    .rename({"obs_value": "adol_pop_thousands", "time_period": "year"})
)

# ── 3. Metadata: select key columns ──────────────────────────────────────────
meta_clean = (
    meta
    .rename({
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)": "life_expectancy",
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)": "gdp_per_capita",
        "Population, total": "population"
    })
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "year",
             "life_expectancy", "gdp_per_capita", "population"])
)

# ── 4. Join all three on country + year ──────────────────────────────────────
combined = (
    neo
    .join(adol, on=["country", "alpha_3_code", "year"], how="left")
    .join(meta_clean, on=["country", "alpha_3_code", "year"], how="left")
)

# ── 5. Most-recent year snapshot (2022) for map & bar ────────────────────────
latest_year = 2022
snap = (
    combined
    .filter(pl.col("year") == latest_year)
    .filter(pl.col("neonatal_deaths").is_not_null())
    .sort("neonatal_deaths", descending=True)
)

print(f"Countries in {latest_year} snapshot: {snap.shape[0]}")
print(snap.head(10).select(["country", "neonatal_deaths", "adol_pop_thousands"]))
Countries in 2022 snapshot: 199
shape: (10, 3)
┌─────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────┬────────────────────┐
│ country                         ┆ neonatal_deaths ┆ adol_pop_thousands │
│ ---                             ┆ ---             ┆ ---                │
│ str                             ┆ i64             ┆ f64                │
╞═════════════════════════════════╪═════════════════╪════════════════════╡
│ India                           ┆ 422440          ┆ 255846.052         │
│ Pakistan                        ┆ 263922          ┆ 54570.352          │
│ Nigeria                         ┆ 253883          ┆ 53224.049          │
│ Ethiopia                        ┆ 114175          ┆ 29224.918          │
│ Congo, the Democratic Republic… ┆ 110371          ┆ 23495.597          │
│ Bangladesh                      ┆ 62847           ┆ 33845.594          │
│ Afghanistan                     ┆ 51406           ┆ 9832.419           │
│ Indonesia                       ┆ 49081           ┆ 46925.882          │
│ Tanzania, United Republic of    ┆ 48609           ┆ 15079.604          │
│ Sudan                           ┆ 41963           ┆ 11392.591          │
└─────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────┴────────────────────┘

3 Where Are Newborns Dying? — World Map

The map below shows neonatal deaths in 2022 across the globe. The deeper the colour, the higher the death toll. Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia emerge as the epicentres of this crisis — countries like Nigeria, India, and the Democratic Republic of Congo account for a disproportionate share of global neonatal deaths.

Code 
import urllib.request, zipfile, io, os

# Download Natural Earth 110m countries shapefile (small ~400 KB zip)
NE_URL = (
    "https://naturalearth.s3.amazonaws.com/110m_cultural/"
    "ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip"
)
zip_path = "/tmp/ne_countries.zip"
if not os.path.exists("/tmp/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.shp"):
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(NE_URL, zip_path)
    with zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path, "r") as z:
        z.extractall("/tmp/")

world_geo = gpd.read_file("/tmp/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.shp")

snap_pd = snap.to_pandas()

# Natural Earth uses ADM0_A3 for ISO alpha-3
world_merged = world_geo.merge(
    snap_pd[["alpha_3_code", "neonatal_deaths"]],
    left_on="ADM0_A3", right_on="alpha_3_code",
    how="left"
)

# Explode geometry into coordinate rows for plotnine geom_polygon
coords_list = []
for idx, row in world_merged.iterrows():
    geom = row["geometry"]
    val  = row["neonatal_deaths"]
    gid  = str(idx)
    if geom is None:
        continue
    geoms = [geom] if geom.geom_type == "Polygon" else list(geom.geoms)
    for poly_i, poly in enumerate(geoms):
        xs, ys = poly.exterior.coords.xy
        for x, y in zip(xs, ys):
            coords_list.append({
                "long": x, "lat": y,
                "group": f"{gid}_{poly_i}",
                "neonatal_deaths": val
            })

map_df = pd.DataFrame(coords_list)

map_plot = (
    ggplot(map_df, aes(x="long", y="lat", group="group", fill="neonatal_deaths"))
    + geom_polygon(color="white", size=0.1)
    + scale_fill_distiller(
        palette="YlOrRd", direction=1,
        name="Neonatal\nDeaths",
        na_value="#d9d9d9",
        breaks=[5_000, 50_000, 150_000, 300_000],
        labels=["5 k", "50 k", "150 k", "300 k"]
    )
    + labs(
        title="Global Neonatal Deaths (2022)",
        subtitle="Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia bear the heaviest burden",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Neonatal Deaths Indicator"
    )
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=16, weight="bold", ha="center"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=11, color="#555555", ha="center"),
        legend_title=element_text(size=9),
        legend_position="right",
        figure_size=(12, 6)
    )
)
map_plot

4 The Top 10 Burden Countries — Bar Chart

Looking more precisely at the ten countries with the highest neonatal death counts in 2022 reveals a stark concentration of suffering. Together, these ten nations account for the majority of all neonatal deaths globally. Nigeria and India lead by a wide margin, each recording over 200,000 neonatal deaths in a single year.

Code 
UNICEF_BLUE = "#1CABE2"
UNICEF_DARK = "#374EA2"

top10 = snap_pd.nlargest(10, "neonatal_deaths").copy()
top10["neonatal_deaths_k"] = top10["neonatal_deaths"] / 1_000
top10 = top10.sort_values("neonatal_deaths_k")

bar_plot = (
    ggplot(top10, aes(x="reorder(country, neonatal_deaths_k)", y="neonatal_deaths_k"))
    + geom_col(fill=UNICEF_BLUE, width=0.7)
    + coord_flip()
    + geom_text(
        aes(label="neonatal_deaths_k.round(1).astype(str) + ' k'"),
        ha="left", size=9, nudge_y=2, color="#333333"
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(expand=(0, 0, 0.15, 0))
    + labs(
        title="Top 10 Countries by Neonatal Deaths (2022)",
        subtitle="Nigeria and India account for more deaths than the next eight combined",
        x=None,
        y="Neonatal Deaths (thousands)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Neonatal Deaths Indicator"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        axis_text=element_text(size=10),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        figure_size=(10, 6)
    )
)
bar_plot

5 Adolescent Population vs. Neonatal Deaths — Scatterplot

Is there a relationship between a country’s adolescent population size and its neonatal death count? The scatterplot below, fitted with a linear regression line, suggests a clear positive association: countries with larger adolescent populations tend to record higher neonatal deaths. This is partly a scale effect — larger populations produce more births and consequently more deaths — but it also reflects that high-fertility nations with large youth cohorts frequently lack the healthcare infrastructure needed to protect newborns.

Code 
scatter_df = snap_pd.dropna(
    subset=["adol_pop_thousands", "neonatal_deaths", "population"]
).copy()

scatter_df["pop_millions"]  = scatter_df["population"] / 1_000_000
scatter_df["adol_millions"] = scatter_df["adol_pop_thousands"] / 1_000
scatter_df["neo_k"]         = scatter_df["neonatal_deaths"] / 1_000

label_countries = [
    "Nigeria", "India", "Pakistan", "Ethiopia",
    "Democratic Republic of the Congo", "China", "Indonesia"
]
scatter_df["label"] = scatter_df["country"].where(
    scatter_df["country"].isin(label_countries), ""
)

scatter_plot = (
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="adol_millions", y="neo_k",
                           size="pop_millions", label="label"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.55, color=UNICEF_BLUE)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color=UNICEF_DARK, fill="#a8b4e8", alpha=0.25, size=1.2)
    + geom_text(ha="left", nudge_x=0.5, size=8, color="#222222")
    + scale_size_continuous(range=(2, 14), name="Population\n(millions)")
    + labs(
        title="Adolescent Population vs. Neonatal Deaths (2022)",
        subtitle="Larger youth cohorts correlate with higher neonatal death burdens",
        x="Adolescent Population (millions, ages 10–19)",
        y="Neonatal Deaths (thousands)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Indicators 1 & 2 + Metadata"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        figure_size=(10, 7)
    )
)
scatter_plot

6 Time-Series: Progress Over Decades

Progress has been made. The time-series below tracks neonatal deaths for the five highest-burden countries from 1988 to 2022. India, in particular, demonstrates a remarkable downward trend from the early 2000s onward — evidence that investment in maternal and newborn healthcare does work. Nigeria, however, tells a more troubling story: deaths have remained stubbornly high, and in some years have even increased, reflecting stalled progress in sub-Saharan Africa’s most populous nation.

Code 
top5_countries = snap_pd.nlargest(5, "neonatal_deaths")["country"].tolist()

ts_df = (
    combined
    .filter(pl.col("country").is_in(top5_countries))
    .filter(pl.col("neonatal_deaths").is_not_null())
    .select(["country", "year", "neonatal_deaths"])
    .to_pandas()
)
ts_df["neo_k"] = ts_df["neonatal_deaths"] / 1_000

PALETTE = ["#1CABE2", "#E2231A", "#FFC20E", "#80BD41", "#374EA2"]

ts_plot = (
    ggplot(ts_df, aes(x="year", y="neo_k", color="country"))
    + geom_line(size=1.1)
    + geom_point(size=1.4, alpha=0.7)
    + scale_color_manual(values=PALETTE, name="Country")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(1990, 2025, 5)))
    + labs(
        title="Neonatal Deaths Over Time: Top 5 Burden Countries (1988–2022)",
        subtitle="India shows clear improvement; Nigeria's toll has plateaued at an unacceptably high level",
        x="Year",
        y="Neonatal Deaths (thousands)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Neonatal Deaths Indicator"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        legend_title=element_text(size=9),
        axis_text_x=element_text(angle=45, ha="right"),
        figure_size=(11, 6)
    )
)
ts_plot

7 Conclusion

The data paint a picture that is simultaneously hopeful and deeply concerning:

  • Progress is real. Countries like India have demonstrated that sustained investment in healthcare infrastructure can dramatically reduce neonatal deaths over time.
  • The burden is concentrated. Just ten countries — nearly all in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia — account for the vast majority of global neonatal deaths.
  • Scale matters, but so does quality of care. The strong correlation between adolescent population size and neonatal deaths is not inevitable; it reflects gaps in healthcare access that can be closed.
  • Nigeria demands urgent attention. As the continent’s most populous nation, stalled progress there has outsized consequences for global statistics.

Every neonatal death is preventable with the right interventions — skilled birth attendants, clean facilities, and accessible antenatal care. The world has both the knowledge and the means to close this gap. What is needed is sustained political will and investment.

Report prepared using UNICEF global indicator data. Visualisations produced with plotnine in Python, rendered with Quarto.
